title: Fever, headache, and altered mental status in an asplenic young adult on lithium and isotretinoin

chart.oneLiner: 22M college student — splenectomized after a childhood trauma, on lithium for bipolar disorder and isotretinoin for acne, heavy recent ibuprofen use — presents with ~18 hours of high fever, worsening headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, vomiting, and new confusion.

opening: My head is pounding, the light is killing me, my neck won't move… and honestly I feel kind of foggy and sick to my stomach.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 22-year-old man, ill-appearing, photophobic, increasingly drowsy and confused. Becomes a poorer historian as he tires; gives short, vague answers and sometimes loses the thread of the question.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student explicitly asks roommates / family / the chart / exposures): Rapidly progressive over ~18h — fever, pounding headache, stiff neck, photophobia, two episodes of vomiting, and new confusion noticed by roommates this morning. Lives in a college dormitory; a hallmate was hospitalized last week with a 'bad infection.' NOT vaccinated against meningococcus or pneumococcus despite having had his spleen removed (only if asked). No recent travel, no sick animals, no IV drug use. No head trauma, no seizure witnessed, no recent dental work.

HPI (from patient — limited): severe global headache that came on over hours and kept getting worse (NOT a single thunderclap maximal-at-onset 'worst headache of my life'); light hurts; neck stiff; threw up twice; feels foggy. He thinks the headache 'might be from my acne pill' because the pharmacist warned him about headaches.
PMH (reveal only when asked): SPLENECTOMY at age 11 after a bicycle/car accident (ruptured spleen). Bipolar I disorder, stable. Severe nodulocystic acne. Mild seasonal allergies. He has had a few 'pseudotumor' headaches blamed on the acne pill before, but never with fever or a stiff neck.
MEDS (reveal only when asked): lithium (takes it 'most days,' missed a couple of doses this week because he felt off and was drinking less water than usual); isotretinoin (started ~6 weeks ago for acne); ibuprofen 600–800 mg several times a day for the past 3 days for the headache; loratadine PRN. NOT on any vaccines/penicillin prophylaxis for his asplenia. No anticoagulants. ALLERGIES: none known.
SOCIAL: college sophomore, lives in a dorm, social alcohol on weekends, no tobacco, no recreational drugs. FAMILY: noncontributory.
ROS: fever, chills, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, nausea/vomiting, confusion. NO focal weakness, NO double vision, NO rash that he has noticed, NO cough or urinary symptoms that he reports. Decreased oral intake and urine output over the last day.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill, toxic, photophobic, lethargic and intermittently confused; dry mucous membranes.
- Vitals: BP 104/64, HR 118, RR 22, 39.4°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Neck: NUCHAL RIGIDITY; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs if tested.
- Neuro: lethargic, oriented only to self, follows simple commands inconsistently; NO clear focal motor deficit; NO pronator drift; pupils equal and reactive; cranial nerves grossly intact; no papilledema described.
- Skin: scattered non-blanching PETECHIAE on the trunk and lower legs with a few early PURPURIC spots if examined; well-healed left upper-quadrant surgical (splenectomy) scar.
- Mucous membranes / turgor: dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor (volume depletion).
- Cardiac/lungs: tachycardic, regular; lungs with faint right basal crackles if auscultated, no respiratory distress.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Provide COLLATERAL (dorm/hallmate exposure, vaccination status, asplenia timeline, urine output) only when the student explicitly asks roommates/chart/exposures or the relevant question.
- Reveal SPLENECTOMY, the medication list (lithium, isotretinoin, heavy ibuprofen), and the missed lithium doses / low fluid intake only when asked about past history, surgeries, or medications.
- Meningeal signs, the petechial/purpuric rash, dry membranes/turgor, and the basal crackles are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- Do NOT volunteer that the headache is gradual-onset rather than thunderclap unless asked how it started and evolved.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.BloodCultures: Two sets drawn immediately before antibiotics; preliminary Gram stain from one bottle later flags Gram-negative diplococci.
labs.CBC: WBC 21.4 with marked left shift and toxic granulation; Hgb 13.8; PLATELETS LOW at 88 (down from a normal baseline).
labs.BMP: Na 128 (LOW), K 6.1 (HIGH), Cl 96, HCO3 16 (LOW); anion gap = 128 − (96 + 16) = 16 (ELEVATED). BUN 38, Creatinine 2.1 (baseline ~0.9) — ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY. Glucose 112.
labs.Coags: INR 1.6, aPTT mildly prolonged, fibrinogen LOW at 110, D-dimer markedly elevated — a consumptive (early DIC) pattern; lactate 4.2 (elevated, explains much of the anion-gap acidosis).
labs.Lithium_level: Serum lithium 1.6 mEq/L (mildly elevated above 1.2 — consistent with volume depletion / AKI reducing clearance, NOT high enough to fully explain the encephalopathy and not the primary problem).
labs.CT_head: Non-contrast CT head (done first ONLY because of the depressed/altered level of consciousness): no mass, no midline shift, no hydrocephalus, no acute hemorrhage — and specifically NO subarachnoid blood — so it is safe to proceed to LP.
labs.LP_CSF: Lumbar puncture CSF: HIGH opening pressure (~28 cm H2O); turbid fluid; neutrophilic pleocytosis (WBC ~3200, ~90% neutrophils); HIGH protein (~220 mg/dL); LOW glucose (~28 mg/dL, CSF:serum ratio <0.3); Gram stain shows GRAM-NEGATIVE DIPLOCOCCI — a bacterial pattern consistent with Neisseria meningitidis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia (~118 bpm) with TALL, PEAKED, narrow-based symmetric T waves (best seen precordially) and slight PR prolongation — hyperkalemic changes consistent with the K 6.1 from AKI; no QRS widening yet, no acute ST-elevation.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with tall peaked symmetric T waves of early hyperkalemia (K ~6). Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with tall peaked T waves of hyperkalemia
searchTerms: early hyperkalaemia 12 lead tented T waves
ekg.clinicalVignette: A febrile, volume-depleted young man with acute kidney injury and K 6.1 has an ECG recorded during resuscitation.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Right lower lobe / posterior basal airspace opacity with air bronchograms in this obtunded, vomiting patient — consistent with early aspiration / a possible pneumococcal-type lung source; no large effusion, no pneumothorax; heart size normal.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: right lower lobe basal consolidation with air bronchograms (aspiration / lobar pneumonia). Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing right lower lobe / basal consolidation
cxr.clinicalVignette: An obtunded young man who has vomited several times and is being intubated for airway protection has a portable chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and exam, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis). This febrile, confused, asplenic young man on lithium and isotretinoin is genuinely ambiguous — explicitly name THREE must-not-miss causes of fever + headache + altered mental status, and say what single feature most argues FOR your leading diagnosis over the closest competitor.
steps.rubric: Bacterial meningitis (leading & CANT-MISS,7) — favored over the competitors by the combination of meningismus + petechial/purpuric rash + toxic appearance in an UNVACCINATED ASPLENIC host (encapsulated-organism risk). Herpes simplex / viral encephalitis (CANT-MISS,4) — the closest competitor given fever + confusion, but argued against by prominent meningismus, a petechial rash, and absent focal/temporal-lobe or seizure features. Subarachnoid hemorrhage (CANT-MISS,4) — argued against because the headache built up over hours rather than thunderclap-maximal-at-onset, and CT shows no blood. Names THREE must-not-miss causes of fever+HA+AMS (core,3). Meningococcemia / septic shock with DIC (core,2). Brain abscess or a parameningeal source (core,1). Lithium toxicity and isotretinoin-related pseudotumor cerebri as CONFOUNDERS that must be addressed but do not explain the fever/meningismus (core,2). Connects fever + meningismus + AMS + petechial rash + asplenia to bacterial meningitis (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on 'just a lithium/medication headache' and missing meningitis (-3); attributing the whole picture to isotretinoin pseudotumor (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Bacterial meningitis (most likely meningococcal) — a febrile, toxic, confused young man with nuchal rigidity, a non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash, and — critically — surgical asplenia without meningococcal/pneumococcal vaccination, which dramatically raises encapsulated-organism risk. Three must-not-miss causes of fever + headache + altered mental status: (1) Bacterial meningitis, (2) HSV/viral encephalitis, (3) Subarachnoid hemorrhage. The single feature that most favors bacterial meningitis over its closest competitor (HSV encephalitis) is the combination of frank meningismus with a petechial/purpuric rash in an asplenic host — encephalitis is typically a parenchymal picture (behavioral change, seizures, temporal-lobe focality) without a purpuric rash; SAH is argued against because the headache built over hours rather than being thunderclap/maximal-at-onset and the CT shows no blood. Also consider meningococcemia/septic shock with DIC and a parameningeal source/brain abscess. The lithium (mildly supratherapeutic from his AKI) and isotretinoin (which he blames for the headache, a pseudotumor cerebri risk) are real confounders to address but neither explains the fever, meningismus, or rash.

coreActions.item: Explicitly names three must-not-miss causes of fever + headache + altered mental status
Addresses lithium toxicity and isotretinoin pseudotumor as confounders that do not explain the fever/meningismus
Connects fever + meningismus + AMS + petechial rash + asplenia to bacterial meningitis

penalties.item: Anchors on a lithium/medication headache and misses meningitis
Attributes the whole picture to isotretinoin pseudotumor cerebri

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What is your workup, and in what ORDER? (Sequencing matters — antibiotics must not be delayed, but this patient has a depressed level of consciousness.)
steps.rubric: Blood cultures immediately, BEFORE antibiotics but without delaying them (CANT-MISS,4). Lumbar puncture for CSF — opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture (CANT-MISS,5). CT head BEFORE LP IS justified HERE because of the depressed/altered level of consciousness (one of the recognized indications) — but it must NOT delay empiric antibiotics + steroids (3). CBC, BMP (catches the hyponatremia/hyperkalemia/AKI), coagulation studies + lactate/fibrinogen for DIC, lithium level (2). EKG and CXR (1). Recognizes that imaging/LP must NOT delay antibiotics — give them after cultures, before/around the CT (1). PENALTY: routine CT before LP with no indication delaying treatment (-2); sending the LP/CT before drawing blood cultures and starting antibiotics (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: blood cultures FIRST (two sets), then give empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone immediately — do not wait. Because he has a depressed/altered level of consciousness (a recognized indication), get a non-contrast CT head before the LP to exclude a mass/shift, but the CT must not delay the antibiotics already given. Then LP for CSF (opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, ± meningococcal/pneumococcal PCR). Send CBC, BMP (look for the hyponatremia/hyperkalemia/AKI), coagulation studies with fibrinogen/D-dimer and lactate (DIC + perfusion), and a lithium level given his AKI. EKG and CXR.

criticalActions.item: Blood cultures immediately, before antibiotics but without delaying them
Lumbar puncture for CSF — opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture

coreActions.item: CT head before LP, justified by the depressed level of consciousness, without delaying antibiotics
Coagulation studies + fibrinogen / D-dimer / lactate for DIC

penalties.item: Routine CT before LP with no indication, delaying treatment
Sending the LP/CT before drawing blood cultures and starting antibiotics

requiredTests: Blood cultures immediately, before antibiotics but without delaying them
Lumbar puncture for CSF — opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture
contraindicatedTests: Routine CT before LP with no indication, delaying treatment
orderSensitiveActions: Blood cultures immediately, before antibiotics but without delaying them
Routine CT before LP with no indication, delaying treatment

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's 12-lead EKG, taken during resuscitation. Describe your findings and say what metabolic problem they reflect and how it changes your immediate management.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with TALL, PEAKED, narrow-based symmetric T waves (± mild PR prolongation), no QRS widening yet — EARLY HYPERKALEMIA matching the K 6.1 from his AKI. CREDIT: identifies tall/peaked/tented T waves (6); attributes them to hyperkalemia and links it to the AKI on the BMP (4); states this mandates urgent treatment of the hyperkalemia (calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin/dextrose ± albuterol, address the AKI) (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling the tracing normal / dismissing the T waves, or over-reading them as hyperacute ischemic T waves and chasing an MI (-3).
keyFindings: tall peaked tented symmetric T waves
attributes to hyperkalemia from the AKI
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1 from AKI).
commonMistakes: calling the tracing normal sinus and ignoring the peaked T waves
over-reading the peaked T waves as hyperacute ischemic T waves and chasing an MI
missing that the hyperkalemia is from the acute kidney injury
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia (~118 bpm) with tall, peaked, narrow-based symmetric T waves (best in the precordial leads) and only slight PR prolongation, no QRS widening — the picture of EARLY hyperkalemia, matching the K of 6.1 driven by his acute kidney injury (NSAID use, volume depletion, sepsis). This is not ischemia. It mandates urgent treatment: IV calcium to stabilize the myocardium, then insulin + dextrose (± albuterol) to shift potassium, plus correcting the AKI (fluids, stop the ibuprofen) — alongside the meningitis treatment, not instead of it.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1 from AKI).
coreActions: tall peaked tented symmetric T waves
attributes to hyperkalemia from the AKI
states it mandates urgent hyperkalemia treatment (calcium, insulin/dextrose)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / over-reads peaked T waves as ischemia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's chest X-ray, taken after he vomited and became obtunded. Describe your findings and how they fit the overall picture.
steps.rubric: Findings = right lower lobe / posterior basal airspace consolidation with air bronchograms — aspiration in this obtunded, vomiting patient and/or a possible pneumococcal-type pulmonary source. CREDIT: identifies basal airspace consolidation with air bronchograms (4); localizes it to the right lower lobe / posterior base (3); interprets it as aspiration in an obtunded patient and/or a possible bacterial lung source contributing to sepsis (3). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the retrocardiac basal consolidation (-2).
keyFindings: right lower lobe / basal consolidation with air bronchograms
aspiration in an obtunded patient and/or a pulmonary source of sepsis
criticalFindings: Right lower lobe / posterior basal consolidation — aspiration and/or a pulmonary source of sepsis.
commonMistakes: missing a retrocardiac / basal consolidation behind the diaphragm
failing to connect it to aspiration in an obtunded patient
steps.idealAnswer: Right lower lobe / posterior basal airspace consolidation with air bronchograms. In this obtunded, repeatedly vomiting patient this is most consistent with early aspiration, and it may also represent a pneumococcal-type pulmonary source feeding the sepsis. It reinforces the need for airway protection, broad antibiotic coverage, and admission to a monitored/ICU setting.

criticalActions: Right lower lobe / posterior basal consolidation — aspiration and/or a pulmonary source of sepsis.
coreActions: right lower lobe / basal consolidation with air bronchograms
aspiration in an obtunded patient and/or a pulmonary source of sepsis

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the basal consolidation

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The CT shows no blood and no mass; the CSF shows high opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose, and Gram-negative diplococci on Gram stain. Commit to a diagnosis. Name the single test result that discriminates it from your closest competitor, state any residual uncertainty, and name THREE mechanisms by which this patient's surgical asplenia predisposed him to this infection.
steps.rubric: Bacterial meningitis — meningococcal (Neisseria meningitidis), committed (4). Names the DISCRIMINATING result: the CSF bacterial pattern (neutrophils, high protein, LOW glucose, positive Gram stain with Gram-negative diplococci) — which excludes the closest competitor HSV/viral encephalitis (lymphocytic, normal glucose, negative Gram stain) and, with the clean CT, excludes SAH (3). States residual uncertainty / pending data — final culture and sensitivities, meningococcal serogroup/PCR, and that the DIC and AKI still need following (1). Names THREE asplenia mechanisms — loss of splenic filtration/clearance of encapsulated bacteria from blood, loss of splenic antibody (IgM) and opsonin production, and absent/low memory B cells reducing rapid antibody response (so overwhelming encapsulated-organism infection), compounded by being unvaccinated (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Bacterial (meningococcal) meningitis — the CSF shows the classic bacterial pattern: a neutrophilic pleocytosis with high protein, LOW glucose (CSF:serum ratio <0.3), high opening pressure, and Gram-negative diplococci on Gram stain consistent with Neisseria meningitidis. The single discriminating result is that CSF Gram stain / bacterial profile: it rules out the closest competitor, HSV/viral encephalitis (which gives a lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and a negative Gram stain), and the clean non-contrast CT plus the absence of subarachnoid blood rules out SAH. Residual uncertainty: the final culture and sensitivities, meningococcal serogroup/PCR, and the trajectory of the early DIC and AKI are still pending and must be followed. Three mechanisms by which his surgical asplenia predisposed him: (1) loss of the spleen's mechanical filtration and phagocytic clearance of encapsulated bacteria from the bloodstream; (2) loss of splenic production of opsonizing antibody (especially IgM) and complement-activating factors needed to opsonize encapsulated organisms; (3) loss of the spleen's marginal-zone memory B cells, blunting the rapid antibody response — all worsened by his never having received meningococcal/pneumococcal vaccination or penicillin prophylaxis.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? (Timing is critical, and this patient has several concurrent problems.) Address the meningitis, the metabolic derangements, the asplenia, and — once he is stabilizing — what must be in place before he could ever be safely discharged.
steps.rubric: Per MGH meningitis: empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone WITHOUT delay after blood cultures (CANT-MISS,6); dexamethasone with/before the first antibiotic dose (CANT-MISS,3); add ampicillin if Listeria risk applies — here he is <50 and not classically at risk, so ampicillin is optional and acyclovir is added only while HSV is still in play (2). TREAT THE CONCURRENT PROBLEMS: treat the hyperkalemia seen on the EKG — IV calcium then insulin/dextrose ± albuterol (CANT-MISS,3); manage the AKI — IV fluids, STOP ibuprofen, hold/adjust lithium and recheck level, renally dose drugs (3); recognize and support early DIC + sepsis — IV fluids/resuscitation, transfuse if bleeding, monitor fibrinogen/platelets, lactate-guided resuscitation (2); airway protection given obtundation + aspiration on CXR (1). PUBLIC-HEALTH/ASPLENIA: droplet precautions + notify public health + chemoprophylaxis for close contacts; and once recovered, give the overdue meningococcal/pneumococcal/Hib vaccines for asplenia (2). SAFE DISCHARGE / DISPOSITION: admit to ICU now; before any eventual discharge — completion of IV antibiotic course, resolved AKI with normalized K and a safe rechecked lithium level, medication reconciliation (discontinue the offending ibuprofen, reconcile lithium and isotretinoin), asplenia vaccination + patient education on fever precautions/standby antibiotics, and close PCP/ID follow-up (3). PENALTY: delaying antibiotics for the CT/LP (-4); discharging or floor-managing an unstable septic patient with hyperkalemia and DIC (-3); continuing ibuprofen / failing to address the AKI (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: This is a neurologic and septic emergency — do not delay antibiotics for the CT/LP. After blood cultures: give dexamethasone with/just before the first antibiotic dose, then empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone immediately; keep acyclovir on board until HSV encephalitis is excluded (ampicillin for Listeria is not needed in this young, non-immunocompromised patient). Treat the concurrent problems the labs and EKG revealed: for the hyperkalemia (K 6.1 with peaked T waves) give IV calcium to stabilize the myocardium then insulin + dextrose ± albuterol; manage the AKI with IV fluids, STOP the ibuprofen, hold and re-dose lithium and recheck the level (his 1.6 reflects reduced clearance), and renally dose medications; support the early DIC/sepsis with fluid resuscitation guided by lactate, transfuse only if bleeding, and trend platelets/fibrinogen. Protect the airway given his obtundation and the aspiration on CXR. Public health and asplenia: droplet precautions, notify public health and arrange meningococcal chemoprophylaxis for close dorm contacts, and — once recovered — give the overdue meningococcal, pneumococcal, and Hib vaccines plus counseling for his asplenia. Disposition: admit to the ICU now. Safe-discharge planning (only once stabilized): completion of the IV antibiotic course, resolution of the AKI with a normalized potassium and a safe rechecked lithium level, medication reconciliation (stop the ibuprofen, reconcile lithium and isotretinoin), asplenia vaccination and education on fever precautions and standby antibiotics, and close PCP/Infectious Disease follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone immediately after blood cultures
Dexamethasone with/before the first antibiotic dose
Treat the hyperkalemia from the EKG — IV calcium then insulin/dextrose ± albuterol

coreActions.item: Manage the AKI — IV fluids, stop ibuprofen, hold/adjust and recheck lithium, renally dose drugs
Add acyclovir while HSV is in play (ampicillin only if Listeria risk)
Recognize and support early DIC / sepsis — fluids, transfuse if bleeding, trend fibrinogen/platelets, lactate-guided
Droplet precautions + public-health notification + chemoprophylaxis for close contacts + asplenia vaccination once recovered
Airway protection given obtundation and aspiration on CXR
Safe discharge / disposition: ICU now, then med reconciliation, resolved AKI/normal K and safe lithium level, asplenia vaccination + fever-precaution education, close PCP/ID follow-up

bonusActions.item: CT before LP justified here by the depressed level of consciousness, without delaying antibiotics

penalties.item: Discharging or floor-managing an unstable septic patient with hyperkalemia and DIC
Continuing ibuprofen / failing to address the AKI

physicalExamMappings.finding: Neck: NUCHAL RIGIDITY; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs if tested.
Skin: scattered non-blanching PETECHIAE on the trunk and lower legs with a few early PURPURIC spots; well-healed left upper-quadrant splenectomy scar.
General/Neuro: ill, toxic, photophobic; lethargic and oriented only to self, follows commands inconsistently; no focal motor deficit; pupils reactive.
Neuro: no clear focal motor deficit; no pronator drift; cranial nerves grossly intact (argues against a focal/parenchymal process such as encephalitis or abscess).
Dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor — volume depletion (decreased intake, vomiting; contributes to the AKI and reduced lithium clearance).
Faint right basal crackles on auscultation (correlates with the basal consolidation/aspiration on the chest film).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 22M college dormitory resident — splenectomized in childhood and unvaccinated against encapsulated organisms, on lithium and isotretinoin with heavy recent ibuprofen use — presents with ~18h of fever, worsening headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, vomiting, and new confusion. The diagnosis is Bacterial (meningococcal) meningitis, confirmed on CSF (neutrophilic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose, Gram-negative diplococci), reached by separating it from its closest competitors (HSV/viral encephalitis and subarachnoid hemorrhage) and by recognizing the concurrent multi-system derangements — hyponatremia, hyperkalemia and AKI, anion-gap (lactic) acidosis, and early DIC — and the lithium/isotretinoin/NSAID confounders.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neck: NUCHAL RIGIDITY; positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs if tested.
Skin: scattered non-blanching PETECHIAE on the trunk and lower legs with a few early PURPURIC spots; well-healed left upper-quadrant splenectomy scar.
General/Neuro: ill, toxic, photophobic; lethargic and oriented only to self; no focal motor deficit; pupils reactive.
Neuro: no clear focal motor deficit; no pronator drift; cranial nerves grossly intact.
Dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor — volume depletion.
Faint right basal crackles on auscultation.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the toxic febrile young adult with confusion.
Elicit the focused history and collateral, uncovering the asplenia, the lithium/isotretinoin/NSAID list, the missed doses, the dorm exposure, and the absent vaccinations only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted exam to reveal meningismus, the petechial/purpuric rash, the splenectomy scar, volume depletion, and basal crackles.
Build a differential led by Bacterial meningitis while genuinely weighing HSV/viral encephalitis and subarachnoid hemorrhage and setting aside the lithium/isotretinoin confounders.
Order an order-sensitive workup (cultures and antibiotics first; CT-before-LP justified by depressed consciousness) and interpret the hyperkalemia EKG, the aspiration CXR, and the multi-system labs.

Commit to meningococcal meningitis on the CSF, name the discriminating result and the asplenia mechanisms, and manage the meningitis alongside the hyperkalemia, AKI, DIC, public-health prophylaxis, and safe disposition.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a lithium/medication or isotretinoin pseudotumor headache and missing meningitis
Treating the meningitis but ignoring the hyperkalemia, AKI, and DIC
Continuing ibuprofen and failing to address the AKI
Forgetting public-health chemoprophylaxis and the overdue asplenia vaccinations

Treat the hyperkalemia seen on the EKG (calcium, then insulin/dextrose) while resuscitating the AKI and sepsis

Lumbar puncture for CSF — opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with tall, peaked, symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia (K 6.1 from AKI).
Right lower lobe / posterior basal consolidation — aspiration and/or a pulmonary source of sepsis.

Dexamethasone with/before the first antibiotic dose
Treat the hyperkalemia from the EKG — IV calcium then insulin/dextrose ± albuterol

imageInterpretationPearls: Tall, peaked, symmetric T waves in a patient with AKI are early hyperkalemia — stabilize the myocardium first, do not chase an MI.
An integrated study is part of the patient's puzzle — read it, then act on it (treat the K, protect the airway).
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Elicits the patient's concerns / ICE (e.g., his worry that it is the acne pill)
Signposts and structures the encounter; uses collateral appropriately
